case_name: Temic removal from surface water for municipal drinking water production
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for Aldicarb-contaminated surface water intended for drinking water supply.

input_data.scenario_background: A small municipal utility draws from a reservoir influenced by agricultural runoff and has detected Temic in the raw water during the growing season. The utility needs a reliable treatment approach to produce drinking water and prefers established processes that can be operated stably by conventional plant staff. Moderate capital and operating costs are acceptable, but the utility wants to avoid unnecessarily high-energy or highly specialized treatment systems.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should achieve reliable Aldicarb control for drinking water compliance while also providing normal surface-water clarification and disinfection.
secondary_goals: maintain stable and straightforward plant operation
integrate well with conventional surface-water treatment

engineering_constraints.residuals_management: conventional sludge handling is available and periodic carbon replacement is manageable

core_unit_functions.coagulation: destabilize particles and reduce natural organic matter load
core_unit_functions.flocculation: form settleable flocs from suspended material
core_unit_functions.sedimentation: remove bulk solids before polishing steps
core_unit_functions.filtration: remove remaining suspended solids and protect downstream adsorptive media
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon: adsorb Aldicarb and provide micropollutant polishing
core_unit_functions.disinfection: ensure microbiological safety of finished drinking water

applicability_rationale: Aldicarb is a small organic pesticide for which activated carbon is a well-established drinking water control option
surface water with moderate turbidity and DOC benefits from upfront clarification to protect carbon performance
the train is compatible with conventional municipal surface-water treatment practice
this option balances treatment reliability, moderate energy use, and stable operation
potential_risks: high DOC or seasonal loading can shorten GAC bed life and increase replacement frequency
breakthrough of Aldicarb requires regular monitoring and timely carbon changeout or reactivation
poor upstream solids removal can foul the carbon beds

core_unit_functions.coagulation: reduce suspended solids and part of the organic load
core_unit_functions.powdered_activated_carbon: provide adsorption of Aldicarb through dose-controlled contact treatment

applicability_rationale: PAC is an established response for episodic pesticide contamination in surface water
the utility can adjust PAC dose during seasonal Temic events without installing a high-energy barrier process
this option fits plants already using conventional clarification and filtration
potential_risks: PAC performance depends strongly on contact time, dose, and competition from natural organic matter
continuous or long-duration contamination can make PAC less economical than GAC
carbon handling and dust control add operational requirements

core_unit_functions.coagulation: remove suspended matter and reduce fouling load
core_unit_functions.advanced_oxidation: oxidize Aldicarb and other oxidizable organic contaminants
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon: adsorb residual parent compound and oxidation byproducts

applicability_rationale: AOP plus GAC can provide strong control where additional assurance for organic micropollutants is desired
the downstream GAC can capture residuals and byproducts from oxidation
this is technically robust but more complex than necessary for a straightforward single-contaminant case
potential_risks: oxidation performance depends on oxidant dose, contact, and water matrix effects
byproduct formation and quenching requirements must be managed
adds chemical and control complexity beyond the simplest proven option

core_unit_functions.coagulation: lower particulate and organic fouling burden ahead of membranes
core_unit_functions.nanofiltration_or_reverse_osmosis: provide a high-barrier separation step for dissolved contaminants
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust finished water chemistry after membrane treatment

applicability_rationale: high-pressure membranes can provide broad contaminant reduction capability
they may be considered where multiple dissolved contaminants are present or a very high barrier is desired
for a single Aldicarb case under moderate constraints, this is more intensive than typically needed
potential_risks: concentrate management creates a significant residuals burden
membrane fouling control and skilled operation are required
capital and energy demands are relatively high for this application

core_unit_functions.coagulation: remove particulate matter prior to downstream treatment
core_unit_functions.air_stripping: transfer volatile contaminants from water to air

applicability_rationale: included as a lower-ranked contrast option rather than a preferred Aldicarb treatment
conventional front-end treatment would still be needed for surface-water potabilization
Aldicarb is not typically addressed by volatilization-based treatment, so support is weak
potential_risks: ineffective for a nonvolatile pesticide such as Aldicarb
could leave the primary contaminant inadequately treated despite otherwise acceptable plant operation
selection would not align with established drinking water guidance for pesticide removal

preferred_rank_pattern: activated_carbon_based_process_chains should rank at the top for Aldicarb in drinking water
conventional clarification should support but not replace the main Aldicarb removal barrier
high_pressure_membrane options should rank below carbon-based conventional solutions under moderate constraints

must_reflect: moderate cost constraint favors established carbon-based treatment over unnecessarily intensive barriers
stable operation required favors process chains compatible with conventional surface-water plant operation
surface-water solids and DOC should be addressed to protect Aldicarb removal performance

must_contain: why activated carbon is appropriate for Aldicarb as a synthetic organic contaminant
why conventional coagulation-sedimentation-filtration is useful as pretreatment for surface water
why membrane or advanced oxidation options rank lower when a simpler proven option is available

should_mention: limitations of treatment processes that are not well matched to nonvolatile pesticides
